headline: Berlin, Hiroshima, San Francisco: the year the world war ended and the world order began
summary: 1945 was the year the deadliest war in history ended and the postwar world was framed. Soviet armies took Berlin and Hitler killed himself on 30 April; Germany signed unconditional surrenders on 7-8 May. On 6 and 9 August the United States dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki; the Soviet Union declared war on Japan on 8 August; Emperor Hirohito announced surrender on 15 August. The United Nations Charter was signed at San Francisco on 26 June; the Bretton Woods institutions began operations. In Yalta and Potsdam the Big Three redrew Europe. Ho Chi Minh proclaimed the Democratic Republic of Vietnam on 2 September; Sukarno declared Indonesian independence on 17 August. A world with new empires, new borders, decolonisation stirring, and the nuclear age was being born from the rubble.

snapshots.text: Europe in 1945 lived the endgame of Hitler's war. On 27 January the Red Army liberated the Auschwitz-Birkenau death camp, one of the last stops of the industrial genocide of six million Jews. From 4 to 11 February Roosevelt, Churchill and Stalin met at Yalta to divide occupation zones and plan the United Nations. The Rhine was crossed at Remagen on 7 March; the Red Army began its Berlin Offensive on 16 April; on 25 April American and Soviet troops met at Torgau on the Elbe. Adolf Hitler shot himself in the Fuehrerbunker on 30 April; Berlin surrendered on 2 May; General Alfred Jodl signed the unconditional surrender at Reims on 7 May, ratified in Berlin on 8-9 May, VE Day. From 17 July to 2 August Truman, Churchill (replaced by Attlee mid-conference after the Labour landslide of 5 July), and Stalin met at Potsdam. Twenty-four surviving Nazi leaders were indicted at Nuremberg on 20 November for the first international trial of aggressive war. Some 40 million Europeans were dead; tens of millions were displaced. The Iron Curtain, in Churchill's phrase, was already descending.

The United States in 1945 shifted from war leadership to superpower institution-building in the space of eight months. Franklin D. Roosevelt died at Warm Springs, Georgia, on 12 April, midway through his fourth term; Vice President Harry S. Truman took the oath the same evening. From 25 April to 26 June representatives of fifty nations met at the San Francisco Conference in the Opera House and Veterans Building to draft the Charter of the United Nations, signed on 26 June. Truman authorised the use of the atomic weapons developed at Los Alamos under J. Robert Oppenheimer and General Leslie Groves; the Trinity test on 16 July preceded Hiroshima and Nagasaki. VJ Day on 15 August ended a war that had cost the US some 400,000 dead. Congress ratified the Bretton Woods agreements creating the IMF and the World Bank in July. In Canada Prime Minister Mackenzie King's Liberals won the 11 June election. In Argentina Colonel Juan Peron survived a coup attempt in October and campaigned for the February 1946 election. In Brazil the Vargas Estado Novo collapsed and general elections in December brought General Eurico Dutra to power.

The Pacific War ended in East Asia in cataclysm. American bombing of Japanese cities under General Curtis LeMay had killed hundreds of thousands; the firebombing of Tokyo on 9-10 March alone killed some 100,000 people. Iwo Jima fell in March, Okinawa in June after 12 weeks of fighting that killed some 200,000 including a third of Okinawa's civilians. On 6 August the Enola Gay dropped the Little Boy uranium bomb on Hiroshima, killing perhaps 80,000 instantly; on 9 August Fat Man was dropped on Nagasaki, killing perhaps 40,000 instantly. The Soviet Union declared war on Japan on 8 August and swept through Manchuria. On 15 August Emperor Hirohito's Jewel Voice Broadcast announced acceptance of the Potsdam Declaration; formal surrender followed on the deck of USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay on 2 September. American occupation under General Douglas MacArthur began. In China the civil war between the Kuomintang under Chiang Kai-shek and the Communists under Mao Zedong resumed. Ho Chi Minh, having led the Viet Minh's August Revolution in Hanoi after the Japanese surrender, proclaimed the Democratic Republic of Vietnam on 2 September at Ba Dinh Square. Korea was partitioned at the 38th parallel between Soviet and American occupation zones.

British India in 1945 emerged from the war a nation on the brink of independence and partition. Some 2.5 million Indians had served in the Second World War, the largest volunteer army in history; famine had killed perhaps three million in Bengal in 1943-44. In the July general election Clement Attlee's Labour Party swept to power in Britain committed to Indian self-government. The Simla Conference of June-July, called by Viceroy Lord Wavell, failed over Muhammad Ali Jinnah's insistence that the Muslim League alone represent Indian Muslims. The Indian National Army trials of officers who had fought under Subhas Chandra Bose alongside the Japanese opened at the Red Fort on 5 November and made national heroes of the defendants. Congress and the Muslim League swept the November-December central legislative elections along communal lines. In Ceylon the Soulbury Commission recommended dominion status. In Persia, occupied since 1941 by Britain and the Soviet Union, Soviet-backed Azerbaijan and Kurdish autonomous republics were being proclaimed. In Afghanistan Zahir Shah's neutral kingdom absorbed refugees. In the Central Asian Soviet republics, deportations of Chechens, Ingush, Crimean Tatars, Volga Germans and other peoples in 1943-44 had killed many hundreds of thousands.

The Middle East in 1945 saw the wartime mandate system beginning to unravel. The Arab League was founded on 22 March in Cairo by Egypt, Iraq, Transjordan, Lebanon, Syria, Saudi Arabia and Yemen, in a treaty signed by the Egyptian prime minister Mustafa el-Nahhas Pasha. On 14 February President Roosevelt met King Abdul Aziz ibn Saud aboard USS Quincy in the Great Bitter Lake, laying the foundation of the US-Saudi oil relationship. In May and June French bombardment of Damascus in an attempt to reassert control over Syria and Lebanon provoked British intervention and effectively ended French rule in the Levant; Syria and Lebanon became independent by year's end. In British-mandate Palestine the Zionist movement, under David Ben-Gurion and Chaim Weizmann, and Palestinian Arab nationalists confronted a Labour government in London that quickly reneged on election-time promises to open immigration; the King David Hotel bombing was still a year off, but the Jewish insurgency intensified with the Hebrew Resistance Movement. In Turkey Ismet Inonu's single-party rule began liberalising in the wake of allied victory. In Iran Prime Minister Mohsen Sadr governed under Anglo-Soviet occupation.

Colonial Africa in 1945 emerged from the war economically transformed and politically restless. The Fifth Pan-African Congress met in Manchester from 15 to 21 October under the presidency of W. E. B. Du Bois, with Kwame Nkrumah as joint political secretary, Jomo Kenyatta of Kenya, George Padmore of Trinidad, Peter Abrahams of South Africa and Obafemi Awolowo of Nigeria in attendance; its resolutions demanded independence and adopted for the first time a language of mass action. The Union of South Africa under Jan Smuts, still an imperial statesman and one of the drafters of the UN Charter, had fought on the Allied side; but 1946 would bring the African Mineworkers' Strike, and 1948 the National Party's apartheid victory. In French West Africa the Brazzaville Conference of 1944 had promised limited reform. In British East Africa demobilised King's African Rifles veterans went home to political ferment. Ethiopia under Emperor Haile Selassie, restored in 1941, negotiated the terms of the disposal of former Italian East Africa. In Egypt strikes and demonstrations against continued British occupation intensified. In Algeria on VE Day, 8 May, French police fired on Muslim demonstrators at Setif and Guelma; the ensuing massacre killed thousands of Algerians and marked, in retrospect, the start of the Algerian revolution.

Australia and New Zealand ended their war on 15 August 1945, VJ Day, having lost some 39,000 and 12,000 killed respectively. Australian Prime Minister John Curtin, who had led the country through Japanese threat and the pivotal turn towards the United States under General Douglas MacArthur, died in office on 5 July; Frank Forde briefly succeeded him before Ben Chifley became Labor leader and prime minister on 13 July. Chifley took Australia into the founding of the United Nations and the Bretton Woods institutions and began postwar reconstruction and immigration schemes. New Zealand's Labour government under Peter Fraser played a proud role at San Francisco. In the mandated territories of Papua and New Guinea Australia resumed administration after the ferocious fighting on the Kokoda track and at Buna, Gona and Rabaul. The former Japanese-mandated Micronesian islands passed to American Trust Territory administration; Guam and Saipan bore the scars of savage 1944 battles. Across Melanesia and Polynesia the war had transformed local economies through American bases and cargo; the cargo cults of Vanuatu and elsewhere entered a new phase. In Indonesia (still formally Netherlands East Indies) Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta proclaimed independence on 17 August, two days after the Japanese surrender.
